# UC-10.12.56 · Clinical Data Exfiltration via Bulk Record Export

## Description

Aggregates audited export-like actions per user—including total rows moved, repeating job counts, and breadth of affected patients—to reveal bulk withdrawals that stray from bedside single-chart workflows.

## Value

Security and integrity teams can intervene before spreadsheets of protected health information linger on unrecognized laptops or cloud drives, shortening breach-notification windows and safeguarding research agreements that limit reuse.

## Implementation

Ensure audits capture `record_count`, `mrn`, and fine-grained `action` classifications from each vendor connector. Populate `record_count` with authoritative numbers from APIs instead of brittle log scraping when possible. Integrate exclusions for sanctioned reporting service accounts keyed on `user_id`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Accurate ingestion of Epic printer and mass-report transcripts plus Cerner `report_run` payloads with normalized `action` labels.
• Governance list of robotic accounts allowed to burst above transactional thresholds stored as `authorized_bulk_accounts.csv` for optional lookup exclusions.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Preserve chain-of-custody by writing raw vendor JSON to cold storage replicas. Normalize case on `action` values and cast `record_count` to integers at index time. Enrich logs with workstation identifiers when vendor exports include them.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Start with thresholds from sample weeks, then escalate after privacy sign-off:

```spl
index=ehr sourcetype IN ("epic:audit","cerner:audit") action IN ("export","print","report_run")
| stats count as export_events sum(record_count) as total_records dc(mrn) as unique_patients by user_id user_name action
| where total_records > 500 OR unique_patients > 100
| sort - total_records
```

Understanding this SPL

**Clinical Data Exfiltration via Bulk Record Export** groups repeated sensitive operations per identity and emphasizes either massive row totals or unusually wide patient footprints.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• `stats ... by user_id user_name action` distinguishes export versus print personas.
• `sum(record_count)` approximates aggregated clinical rows when vendors supply counts.
• `dc(mrn)` approximates uniqueness even if totals omit duplicates.

Step 3 — Validate
Replay five highest rows against native audit replay tools. Interview requestors for documented business justification. Correlate workstation asset tags when available.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Pair alerts with playbook steps: revoke tokens, escalate to security operations, initiate privacy workflows. Maintain dashboard trending for sanctioned bulk movers versus suspicious spikes. Consider visualizations: Bar chart totals, Scatter exports versus patients, Table with SLA timers for analyst review.

## SPL

```spl
index=ehr sourcetype IN ("epic:audit","cerner:audit") action IN ("export","print","report_run")
| stats count as export_events sum(record_count) as total_records dc(mrn) as unique_patients by user_id user_name action
| where total_records > 500 OR unique_patients > 100
| sort - total_records
```

## Visualization

Bar chart (total_records by user), Bubble chart (export_events versus unique_patients), Table with action breakdown.

## Known False Positives

Month-end regulatory reporting bundles, retrospective quality studies approved by oversight boards, payer audit responses, scanning rooms printing multi-patient manifests during disasters, and ETL jobs mis-tagged under human user IDs.
